maxwell.headline: Ghislaine Maxwell invoked Fifth Amendment in February 2026 House Oversight deposition, with attorney offering testimony conditional on Trump clemency; habeas corpus petition denied January 2026.
maxwell.summary: On January 13, 2026, a federal judge denied Maxwell's habeas corpus petition filed under 28 U.S.C. section 2255, exhausting her direct legal challenges after the Supreme Court declined her appeal.[1] In February 2026, Maxwell invoked her Fifth Amendment rights during a virtual deposition before the House Oversight Committee, refusing to testify unless granted clemency by President Trump, as stated by her attorney David Oscar Markus.[1][2][4] Markus indicated Maxwell would provide full testimony on Epstein matters, including exonerating Trump and Clinton, if clemency is granted, but lawmakers from both parties rejected the offer and denied immunity.[2][4] No clemency petition has been publicly filed, and Maxwell remains incarcerated at a federal facility with a release date around 2037.[1]

iran.headline: Strait of Hormuz commercial shipping near-total collapse with only Iran-linked vessels transiting; multiple merchant vessel strikes reported; Iranian mine deployment preparations underway
iran.summary: The Strait of Hormuz has experienced severe disruption since February 28, 2026, with commercial traffic reduced to near-standstill levels. As of March 10, only two outbound crossings were recorded with zero inbound movements, representing a 100% increase from the previous day but still far below the seven-day average of 3.29 crossings[1]. Remote sensing intelligence detected eight dark vessels operating under partially visible or dark conditions within the Strait on March 10[1]. Multiple commercial vessels were reportedly struck on March 11, reinforcing kinetic threats to shipping[1]. Iran has begun preparations to deploy naval mines in the Strait, raising escalation risk of temporary closure to all commercial shipping[1]. Only Iran-linked vessels have been observed transiting the waterway over recent days, with the last non-Iranian commercial vessel (Chinese bulk carrier Sino Ocean) crossing on March 8[4]. Storage tanks across the region are rapidly filling and refineries have begun cutting processing rates as oil tankers remain unable to move in or out of the Persian Gulf[4].

gulf_swf.headline: Saudi PIF soft-launches 2026-2030 strategy, shifting focus to AI, industry, minerals, and tourism while scaling back mega-projects like The Line amid fiscal pressures.
gulf_swf.summary: Saudi Arabia's $925 billion Public Investment Fund (PIF) previewed its new five-year strategy to key investors at a Riyadh conference this week, prioritizing sectors such as industry, minerals, artificial intelligence, tourism, logistics, mining, advanced manufacturing, and clean energy.[1][2][4] The plan scales back expensive mega-projects including The Line, Trojena, and a Riyadh skyscraper due to delays, cost overruns, and low oil prices below levels needed for Vision 2030 funding.[1][2] PIF emphasized private sector partnerships, with over SAR 591 billion in local content spending from 2020-2024 and 190+ investment opportunities worth SAR 40 billion offered internationally.[3] No reports confirm Iran strikes on SWF infrastructure, Strait of Hormuz disruptions, or wartime stress impacts on Gulf SWFs like Mubadala, MGX, ADIA, or QIA.

israel.headline: Israel and US conducted over 1,600 IAF sorties into Iran by March 10, destroying 300+ missile launchers and striking Tehran command centers amid ongoing Hezbollah attacks from Lebanon.
israel.summary: The Israel-US coalition launched Operation Roaring Lion on February 28, 2026, with massive airstrikes killing Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei and targeting missile launchers, air defenses, and IRGC sites across Iran, including 1,600 IAF sorties and 4,000 munitions dropped by March 10.[1][2][4][5][7] On March 10, IDF struck Iranian command centers in Tehran and Tabriz, including IRGC and special forces headquarters.[1][5] Hezbollah escalated with multiple attacks on IDF positions in northern Israel and southern Lebanon, prompting IDF strikes on 60 Hezbollah targets and ground force deployments along the border as of March 3.[2][3] No new developments reported on Unit 8200, NSO Group, Abraham Accords investments, or Board of Peace operations.

oracle_ellison.headline: Oracle stock price rose 9.18% to close at $163.12 on March 11, 2026, with market cap at $454.99B as of March 12.
oracle_ellison.summary: Oracle's stock exhibited volatility in early March 2026, closing at $163.12 on March 11 after a 9.18% gain on high volume of 82.5 million shares[1]. Market capitalization stood at $454.99B on March 12, down from prior peaks but stable among top software firms[2]. No reports confirm financial stress, $45-50B capital raise, Stargate Abilene expansion cancellation, bondholder lawsuits, or Larry Ellison's $40.4B WBD personal guarantee[1][2][3][4][5].

epstein_files.headline: DOJ released three previously withheld FBI 302 reports naming Trump in uncorroborated Epstein allegations on March 5, 2026, amid accusations of withholding.
epstein_files.summary: The DOJ released three missing FBI 302 interview summaries on March 5, 2026, related to a 2019 accuser's uncorroborated claims of abuse by Trump as a minor, after initially claiming they were duplicative.[1][2] Senator Sheldon Whitehouse demanded preservation of remaining withheld records, including 37 pages of FBI notes, accusing the MAGA DOJ of a cover-up.[1] Earlier, on January 30, DOJ claimed full compliance with the Epstein Files Transparency Act but released only one of four 302s; public pressure led to the March disclosure.[1][2] Additional hundreds of pages, including prosecutor memos from 2007, were also published.[2]

arkansas_datacenter.headline: AVAIO Digital announced $6B initial investment for AI-ready data center campus in Pulaski County, Arkansas, on January 12, 2026, with construction starting Q1 2026.
arkansas_datacenter.summary: AVAIO Digital revealed plans for AVAIO Digital Leo, a 760-acre data center hub near Little Rock, marking the largest economic investment in Arkansas history, potentially scaling to $21B total.[1][2] The project includes a contract with Entergy Arkansas for 150 MW initial power, expandable to 1 GW, supported by Act 373 incentives and reduced regulatory timelines praised by Governor Sarah Huckabee Sanders.[1][2][3] Construction begins in Q1 2026, with Phase 1 completion by June 2027, creating thousands of construction jobs and over 500 permanent positions.[2][4] Reports also link Google to a separate $1B data center at Little Rock Port.[5][7]

convergence_note: 8 nodes active simultaneously — within the 7-day convergence window defined by the thermostat model.
